title: Human Dignity & Rights
statement: We are committed to developing and deploying AI that respects human dignity, autonomy, and fundamental rights. No AI system will be used in ways that demean, manipulate, or deprive individuals of their rights.

title: Transparency & Explainability
statement: Our AI systems will be transparent about their capabilities and limitations, and subject to meaningful human oversight. We will provide explanations of AI-driven decisions that affect individuals to the extent technically feasible.

title: Fairness & Non-Discrimination
statement: We will proactively identify, measure, and mitigate bias in our AI systems. AI must not create or reinforce unlawful discrimination on the basis of protected characteristics.

title: Accountability
statement: Clear human accountability for AI decisions is assigned, documented, and enforced. Every AI system has a designated owner responsible for its governance, performance, and risk.

title: Safety First
statement: Safety is a prerequisite for deployment. AI systems that cannot be made safe — through technical controls, human oversight, or operational constraints — will not be deployed in production.

title: Privacy by Design
statement: Privacy is embedded into AI system design from the outset, not bolted on. We minimize data collection, protect personal information, and give individuals meaningful control over their data.

title: Sustainability
statement: We measure and minimize the environmental impact of our AI systems, including compute energy, water usage, and hardware lifecycle. We also design AI for inclusive growth — ensuring benefits are broadly shared and do not exacerbate societal inequalities. Sustainability and well-being are factors in AI procurement and design decisions.

title: Beneficial AI
statement: Our AI systems are designed to create net positive value for customers, employees, and society. We will not develop AI whose primary function is to harm, deceive, or exploit.

title: Robustness & Security
statement: Our AI systems must be technically robust and secure throughout their entire lifecycle. Systems shall function reliably under normal use, foreseeable misuse, and adverse conditions — including adversarial attacks. We implement cybersecurity controls, continuous monitoring, and fallback mechanisms to detect and correct failures before they cause harm.

title: EU AI Act Provider Obligations
statement: As a provider of AI systems placed on the EU market, we comply with all obligations under EU AI Act Articles 16–27. This includes conformity assessment, technical documentation, quality management, post-market monitoring, and incident reporting for high-risk AI systems.

title: EU AI Act Deployer Obligations
statement: As a deployer of high-risk AI systems, we comply with EU AI Act Article 26 obligations: maintaining human oversight, using systems only for intended purposes, ensuring staff competency, and logging operations as required.

title: High-Risk AI System Controls
statement: AI systems classified as high-risk under EU AI Act Annex III require: risk management systems, data governance, technical documentation, transparency to users, human oversight measures, accuracy standards, and cybersecurity controls.

title: AI Risk Governance Policy
statement: We implement an AI risk governance framework aligned with NIST AI RMF GOVERN function. This includes defining AI risk tolerance, establishing roles and responsibilities, creating policies and procedures, and ensuring organizational accountability for AI risk management.

title: AI & HIPAA Compliance
statement: AI systems that access, process, or generate Protected Health Information (PHI) must comply with HIPAA Security Rule §164.308. This includes access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, and transmission security. All AI vendors with PHI access require a Business Associate Agreement (BAA).

title: AI & Fair Credit Reporting
statement: AI systems used in credit decisioning must comply with the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA). Adverse action notices must be provided when AI contributes to credit denials. AI models must not use prohibited data as proxy variables for credit decisions.

title: Model Risk Management
statement: AI/ML models used in financial decision-making must follow model risk management practices per SR 11-7 or equivalent. Requirements include model validation, documentation, ongoing monitoring, and board-level oversight of material model risk.

title: AI & GDPR Obligations
statement: AI systems processing personal data of EU/UK residents must comply with GDPR. Article 22 restrictions on solely automated decision-making apply. Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) are required for high-risk AI processing. Data subjects retain rights to explanation and human review.

title: AI & CCPA/CPRA
statement: AI systems processing personal information of California residents must comply with CCPA/CPRA. This includes opt-out rights for automated decision-making, disclosure of AI use in profiling, and data minimization requirements.

title: FedRAMP AI Requirements
statement: AI systems deployed in or supporting federal government environments must meet FedRAMP Rev 5 authorization requirements. Cloud-based AI services require FedRAMP Moderate or High authorization as appropriate to data classification.

title: Executive Order 14110 Compliance
statement: Federal contractors using AI in scope of EO 14110 must comply with Section 4 requirements: safety evaluations, transparency reports, and red-team testing for dual-use foundation models. AI systems in critical infrastructure must meet sector-specific AI safety requirements.

title: ISO 42001 AI Management System Policy
statement: The organization commits to establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI Management System (AIMS) aligned with ISO 42001. Top management establishes an AI policy appropriate to the organization's purpose and supporting strategic direction.

title: SOC 2 Type II for AI Systems
statement: AI systems and the infrastructure they run on must satisfy SOC 2 Type II Trust Services Criteria, with particular emphasis on CC6 (Logical and Physical Access Controls) and CC7 (System Operations). Annual Type II audits with unqualified opinions are required for customer-facing AI services.

title: ISO 27001 Information Security Management
statement: AI systems and associated data assets are in scope of the ISO 27001:2022 Information Security Management System (ISMS). Controls from Annex A relevant to AI (A.8 Technology Controls, A.5 Organizational Controls) are implemented and audited annually.

title: Model Cards Documentation
statement: Every AI model deployed in production must have a published model card documenting: intended use, out-of-scope uses, training data summary, evaluation results by demographic group, ethical considerations, and known limitations. Model cards are updated at each major version.

title: EU AI Act Conformity Assessment
statement: High-risk AI systems require a conformity assessment before market placement. Assessment must verify compliance with requirements in EU AI Act Articles 9–15 and be documented in a Declaration of Conformity. Third-party assessment required for biometric and critical infrastructure AI.

title: AI Bill of Materials (AIBOM)
statement: All AI systems must maintain an AI Bill of Materials documenting: base models, fine-tuning datasets, third-party components, training libraries, and inference dependencies. AIBOMs follow SPDX or CycloneDX format and are updated at each release.

title: Bias & Fairness Testing Standard
statement: Before production deployment, AI systems used in consequential decisions must pass bias and fairness testing across protected characteristics. Tests must use demographically representative evaluation sets. Results are documented and material disparities addressed before launch.

title: Explainability Documentation Standard
statement: AI systems used for consequential individual decisions must provide post-hoc explanations using approved explainability methods (SHAP, LIME, or equivalent). Explanations must be validated for faithfulness and completeness, documented in technical specifications.

title: AI Incident Response Standard
statement: A documented AI incident response plan must be maintained, tested annually, and activated within 1 hour of confirmed AI system failure or misuse incident. Serious incidents affecting EU citizens are reported to national authorities within 15 days per EU AI Act Art. 73.

title: Training Data Quality Standard
statement: Training and fine-tuning datasets must meet documented quality criteria: completeness, accuracy, representativeness, and currency. Data lineage must be traceable from source to model. PII in training data requires privacy review and lawful basis documentation.

title: Red Team Testing Standard
statement: High-risk and customer-facing AI systems must undergo red team adversarial testing before production deployment and annually thereafter. Testing covers prompt injection, jailbreaks, data extraction, and misuse scenarios using MITRE ATLAS or equivalent methodology.

title: Document AI System Limitations
statement: Document known limitations, failure modes, and out-of-distribution behaviors in model cards and system documentation before each deployment. Limitations should be communicated to end users in plain language.

title: Pre-Deployment Bias Reviews
statement: Conduct a structured bias review before each production deployment of AI systems that affect individuals. Include diverse stakeholders in the review. Document findings and remediation actions.

title: Involve Domain Experts in Data Review
statement: Involve domain experts (clinical, legal, financial as appropriate) in AI training data review and evaluation design. Expert review catches domain-specific biases and quality issues that automated checks miss.

title: Quarterly AI Ethics Reviews
statement: Perform quarterly AI ethics reviews with cross-functional teams including product, legal, engineering, and affected business units. Review covers new AI use cases, incidents, regulatory developments, and emerging risks.

title: Explainability Tools for High-Stakes Decisions
statement: Use explainability tools (SHAP, LIME, attention visualization, or equivalent) for AI systems making high-stakes decisions. Make explanations available to decision-makers and, where legally required, to affected individuals.

title: Catalog Shadow AI Before Integration
statement: Identify and assess all shadow AI (AI tools used without formal approval) before integrating into business processes. Uncatalogued AI use creates unmanaged risk and compliance exposure.

title: Monitor Model Drift
statement: Monitor AI model performance and data drift in production. Define drift thresholds and retraining schedules based on system risk level. Document and review drift events.

title: Disclose AI Use to Affected Individuals
statement: Notify users when they are interacting with an AI system or when an AI system has made or significantly influenced a decision affecting them. Disclosure should be timely, clear, and accessible.

title: Patient Safety First
statement: AI systems used in clinical or care settings must prioritise patient safety above operational efficiency. Any AI recommendation that could affect clinical outcomes must be subject to clinician review before action.

title: Consumer Fairness
statement: AI systems used in credit, insurance, or financial product decisions must treat consumers fairly and equitably. Models must be validated against fair lending and consumer protection standards before deployment.

title: Democratic Accountability
statement: AI systems used in public administration must be subject to democratic oversight and public scrutiny. Automated decision-making affecting citizens must include a meaningful right to human review and appeal.

title: Mandatory Risk Assessment
statement: All AI systems must undergo a structured risk assessment before production deployment. The assessment must classify the system's risk level, identify potential harms, and document mitigating controls.

title: Privacy Law Compliance
statement: AI systems that process personal data must comply with all applicable privacy laws and regulations. Data minimisation, purpose limitation, and subject rights must be respected throughout the AI lifecycle.

title: Named Accountable Owner
statement: A named individual must be designated as the accountable owner for every AI system in production. The owner is responsible for the system's ongoing compliance, performance, and ethical operation.

title: Training Data Access Control
statement: Access to AI training data, model weights, and fine-tuning pipelines is restricted to authorised personnel. Access must be granted on a least-privilege basis and reviewed quarterly.

title: AI Change Management
statement: All AI model updates, retraining runs, and prompt modifications must pass a documented security and quality review before promotion to production. Changes must be tracked in version control.

title: Minimum AI Controls
statement: Minimum required evidence controls for AI systems based on operating model and verticals: MFA on all AI access, structured logging and monitoring active, AI incident response plan documented and tested annually.

title: MFA for All AI System Access
statement: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is required for all human access to AI systems, training infrastructure, model registries, and production APIs. Exceptions require documented waiver approved by CISO or equivalent.

title: Logging & Monitoring on Production AI
statement: Logging and monitoring must be active on all production AI systems. Logs must capture: inputs/outputs (where privacy permits), inference latency, error rates, and access events. Logs are retained for minimum 12 months and reviewed for anomalies.

title: AI Incident Response Plan
statement: A documented AI incident response plan must be maintained and tested at least annually via tabletop exercise. The plan covers: detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident review. Responsible parties are identified and contacts are current.

title: Vendor Security Attestation on File
statement: For each AI vendor or cloud provider with access to organizational data or systems, a current SOC 2 Type II report (or equivalent attestation) must be on file. Attestations must be reviewed annually and before contract renewal.

title: Agentic Guardrails & Human-in-the-Loop
statement: Autonomous and semi-autonomous AI agents must have documented guardrails limiting scope of action and defined human-in-the-loop checkpoints for irreversible or high-impact decisions. Guardrail configurations are version-controlled and change-managed.
